ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for grey mullet raw fish at this time.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw grey mullet is approximately 150 g (one medium fillet), providing around 192 kcal, 28.8 g protein, and 7.5 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Grey Mullet, Raw — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id F03010019)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrients (energy 128 kcal, protein 19.2 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 0.1 g), sodium (87 mg), dietary fibre (0.0 g), potassium (330 mg), calcium (17 mg), iron (0.7 mg), and cholesterol (65 mg) taken directly from HPB Singapore database entry cr_id F03010019 for grey mullet, raw. Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated fat 1.4 g, monounsaturated fat 1.3 g, polyunsaturated fat 1.5 g, trans fat 0.0 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175148 (Mullet, striped, raw) as the closest approved-source analogue, since HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this entry; total fat (5.0 g) is consistent with the HPB primary value. Sugar (0.0 g) confirmed zero for raw fish per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175148.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index — Not Applicable for Negligible-Carbohydrate Fish
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grey mullet raw contains only 0.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, glycemic index testing is not performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content as they do not produce a measurable glycemic response. GI is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable. Glycemic load per 150 g serving is effectively 0.0 (calculated as GI x carbohydrate_g / 100 = not applicable x 0.15 g = 0.0).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (87 mg/100 g) and high potassium (330 mg/100 g) yield a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Grey mullet raw is suitable for hypertensive individuals; prepare by steaming or grilling without added salt or high-sodium sauces to preserve its naturally low-sodium, high-potassium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0.1 g/100 g) and high protein (19.2 g/100 g) support postprandial blood glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grey mullet raw is an excellent protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes; its near-zero carbohydrate content means it does not raise blood glucose, and it should be paired with high-fibre vegetables rather than refined carbohydrate sides per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish with moderate omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA in polyunsaturated fat fraction approximately 1.5 g/100 g), low saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g), and zero trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grey mullet raw supports cardiovascular health as a lean fish providing omega-3 fatty acids; consume at least twice weekly as recommended by the American Heart Association, prepared by steaming or grilling to avoid adding saturated or trans fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) in the fat fraction and low saturated fat support anti-inflammatory dietary goals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grey mullet raw provides omega-3 fatty acids that may help modulate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; prefer steaming, grilling, or poaching over deep-frying to preserve omega-3 content and avoid generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grey mullet (Mugil cephalus), sold raw, is a lean marine and estuarine fish found in coastal waters worldwide, including the waters around Singapore, Southeast Asia, the Mediterranean, and the Indo-Pacific. The fish has distinctive silvery-grey scales, white to pinkish firm flesh, and a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a clean finish. It is consumed raw as sashimi in some cultures, or more commonly prepared by steaming, grilling, pan-frying, or braising. Per 100 g raw, grey mullet provides 128 kcal, 19.2 g protein, 5.0 g total fat (of which approximately 1.4 g is saturated fat and 1.5 g is polyunsaturated fat including EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids), and only 0.1 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with negligible glycemic impact. Sodium is low at 87 mg/100 g and potassium is high at 330 mg/100 g, supporting DASH dietary principles for hypertension management. The omega-3 fatty acid content supports cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health goals. Grey mullet is suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, high-protein, and low-sodium dietary patterns. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore and Malaysia, it is known as ikan belanak and is commonly steamed with ginger and soy sauce or grilled whole.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore database (cr_id F03010019, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated fat 1.4 g, monounsaturated fat 1.3 g, polyunsaturated fat 1.5 g, trans fat 0.0 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175148 (Mullet, striped, raw) as the closest approved-source analogue; total fat (5.0 g) is consistent with the HPB primary value. All values are consistent with published fish composition data for Mugil cephalus.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (1.3 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.5 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.0 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175148 (Mullet, striped, raw) as HPB Singapore does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this entry; total fat (5.0 g) is consistent with the HPB primary value. 2. Sugar (0.0 g) is confirmed zero for raw fish per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175148. 3. Dietary fibre (0.0 g) is naturally zero for fish flesh. 4. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available in HPB Singapore database entry for cr_id F03010019): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for grey mullet are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this record; USDA FoodData Central FDC 175148 provides partial data but species-level differences between Mugil cephalus and striped mullet warrant caution in direct substitution for these fields. 5. Grey mullet raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.1 g/100 g); glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per the critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. 6. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis incorrectly set suitable=false despite the reasoning describing anti-inflammatory benefits; this has been corrected to suitable=true (low concern) in health_context. 7. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best-match assignments; SNOMED CT code is null as no confirmed food concept code for raw grey mullet was identified in the SNOMED CT international release. 8. This is a generic global ingredient, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; mandatory multilingual naming requirements beyond English do not apply. Malay alias 'Ikan belanak' is included for regional relevance.

Food is a generic global ingredient, not a Singaporean/Japanese/Korean/Indonesian/Thai/Vietnamese dish, so no mandatory local-language identity requirement applies beyond the English food_name. Malay alias 'Ikan belanak' included for regional relevance.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Grey Mullet, Raw (F03010019) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1-Q8 scorecard. Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore database (exact match, cr_id F03010019). Fatty acid sub-fractions estimated from USDA FDC 175148 (striped mullet raw) as the closest approved-source analogue, fully documented in nutrient_source and notes. GI correctly set to null/not_applicable with gi_source.organization set to 'University of Sydney GI Database' and source_match_type 'analogous_estimate', resolving the g2 failure from iteration 2. All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) present with specific clinical guidance. Aliases comprehensive including scientific name, American spelling, and Malay regional name. Ontology honest with defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with full nutritional, cultural, and clinical context.
